A programmatic, sampled world-state of entities, relations, and an invented lexicon that persists across documents, replacing per-document local consistency and promoting S3 from a deferred severity rung to the central coordination mechanism.

A non-LLM process samples a world-state, a set of invented entities, their relations, and a shared invented lexicon, and holds it fixed across many documents, so documents about the same or overlapping fragments of the world-state are automatically mutually consistent and can still be specific and varied. The LLM's role is reduced to rendering a given fragment fluently.

This mechanism directly answers the information-is-consistent-novelty problem (node 34) and the diversity-contraction problem (node 35): consistency comes from the shared, program-held state rather than from the LLM, so the LLM is free to render varied local fragments without risking contradiction. It supersedes node 10 (per-document local consistency) for any reasoning-era text and promotes node 25 (S3 invented lexicon) from an isolated severity rung to the mechanism that also carries information content, not just vocabulary. First increment implemented as slm.worldgen: a sampler for small consistent worlds (people, places, objects; residence, workplace, ownership, storage as functions; ages and sizes as total rank orders so every comparison is well-defined), template-based verbalization with varied and direction-inverting surface forms, document emission (world_documents), and binding-task emission with ground-truth answers (node 33). Deliberately template-rendered and single-domain in this pass; the next increments are the LLM-as-stylist rendering layer with round-trip verification (node 38), a persistent world shared across many documents rather than a fresh small world per document, multi-hop question construction, and mixing world documents into the pretraining corpus at a configured fraction.
